perturbation.original_text: RE: (We will complete the name of the business on receipt of this NDA) (“the Business”) You have expressed an interest in discussing the acquisition of the above Business (‘the Proposed Transaction”). This letter of agreement sets out the terms upon which we will supply information to you in respect of the Business in order to enable discussions to take place on an informed basis (“the Confidential Information”).
perturbation.changed_text: RE: (We will complete the name of the business on receipt of this NDA) (“the Business”) You have expressed an interest in discussing the acquisition of the above Entity (‘the Proposed Transaction”). This letter of agreement sets out the terms upon which we will supply information to you in respect of the Business in order to enable discussions to take place on an informed basis (“the Confidential Information”).
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Business' to 'Entity' when referring to the 'Proposed Transaction' creates confusion. While 'Business' is defined, 'Entity' is not, making it unclear if the Proposed Transaction refers to a business sale, an entity transfer, or something else. This is an in-text contradiction because the document uses both terms without clarifying their relationship, contradicting the initial definition of 'Business' for the transaction itself.
perturbation.contradicted_text: RE: (We will complete the name of the business on receipt of this NDA) (“the Business”) You have expressed an interest in discussing the acquisition of the above Entity (‘the Proposed Transaction”). This letter of agreement sets out the terms upon which we will supply information to you in respect of the Business in order to enable discussions to take place on an informed basis (“the Confidential Information”).
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Business' to 'Entity' in the 'Proposed Transaction' definition introduces a contradiction. The original text defines 'Business' but the changed text uses 'Entity' without a definition, even though 'Business' is defined elsewhere. This contradicts the original intended meaning and creates confusion because the contract now uses two potentially different terms ('Business' and 'Entity') interchangeably to describe the subject of the acquisition, which is a Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction. This directly impacts the understanding and enforceability of the contract by introducing ambiguity and conflicting terms regarding the target of the acquisition which the explanation describes, and the definition specifies.

perturbation.original_text: F) to acknowledge that the property and copyright in the Confidential Information belongs to us and that no licence or any other right is granted to you in respect of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: F) to acknowledge that the property and copyright in the Confidential Information belongs to us and that no license or any other right is granted to you in respect of the Protected Data.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Protected Data' in this clause, it creates uncertainty about what rights the recipient has. 'Confidential Information' is defined, but 'Protected Data' is not. This contradicts the purpose of clause F, which is to clearly define the ownership and usage rights related to 'Confidential Information.' The meaning is altered without explicit clarification, creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: F) to acknowledge that the property and copyright in the Confidential Information belongs to us and that no license or any other right is granted to you in respect of the Protected Data.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Protected Data.' The explanation states that 'Confidential Information' is defined elsewhere in the contract, while 'Protected Data' is not. This creates a contradiction because the clause aims to define rights related to 'Confidential Information,' but the change introduces an undefined term, leading to uncertainty and potential conflict in interpretation and enforcement. The changed text and contradicted text are in the same location, however the introduction of a non defined term, where there was a previously defined term, causes a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: G) to destroy immediately on our written request all Confidential Information in your possession including any copies made (save where we are required pursuant to some law or enactment or regulation or the rules of any Stock Exchange to retain originals or copies of any of the Confidential Information).
perturbation.changed_text: G) to destroy immediately on our written request all Confidential Documents in your possession including any copies made (save where we are required pursuant to some law or enactment or regulation or the rules of any Stock Exchange to retain originals or copies of any of the Confidential Information).
perturbation.explanation: Switching from 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Documents' creates ambiguity. While the original text clearly defines 'Confidential Information,' the term 'Confidential Documents' is undefined. This is problematic because the instruction to destroy applies to 'Confidential Documents', however the exception of save where we are required pursuant to some law to retain originals or copies of any of the 'Confidential Information'. This is an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: G) to destroy immediately on our written request all Confidential Documents in your possession including any copies made (save where we are required pursuant to some law or enactment or regulation or the rules of any Stock Exchange to retain originals or copies of any of the Confidential Information).
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Confidential Documents'. The original text defines 'Confidential Information', while 'Confidential Documents' is undefined. The obligation to destroy applies to 'Confidential Documents', however the save clause mentions 'Confidential Information'. Since only Confidential Information is defined, and the save clause only references confidential information, there is an in-text contradiction created.

perturbation.original_text: 5) that any notices under this agreement shall be sent by first-class prepaid post to the address of the recipient party as set out in this letter and shall be deemed to have been received on the second week day (that is, any day excluding Sundays or public or bank holidays in England) following the day on which it was posted.
perturbation.changed_text: 5) that any communications under this agreement shall be sent by first-class prepaid post to the address of the recipient as noted on page one and shall be deemed to have been received on the second week day (that is, any day excluding Sundays or public or bank holidays in England) following the day on which it was posted.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'notices' to 'communications' and 'recipient party' to 'recipient' creates uncertainty. While 'notices' are related to the agreement, 'communications' is broader and undefined, potentially including less formal interactions. Moreover, by not specifying where the addrees is set out and just noting 'as noted on page one' leaves the definition open to interpretation. This results in an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5) that any communications under this agreement shall be sent by first-class prepaid post to the address of the recipient as noted on page one and shall be deemed to have been received on the second week day (that is, any day excluding Sundays or public or bank holidays in England) following the day on which it was posted.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies 'notices' to the broader term 'communications,' which introduces ambiguity, as described in the explanation. The changed text modifies 'recipient party' to 'recipient' and removes the specific place for the recipient address, noting it is simply 'on page one', which introduces ambiguity. The contradictory text also contains the same phrasing, so the ambiguity now exists in two places.

perturbation.original_text: Please note that should you choose to return the NDA via Email, we respectfully request that the original is returned by post.
perturbation.changed_text: Please note that should you choose to return the Agreement via Email, we respectfully request that the original is returned by courier.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'NDA' to 'Agreement' and 'post' to 'courier', we create confusion. 'NDA' is established, and now we change to 'Agreement', without specifying if it's the same. Also, we change 'post' to 'courier', creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Please note that should you choose to return the Agreement via Email, we respectfully request that the original is returned by courier.
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to the return of 'NDA' via 'post'. The changed text refers to the return of 'Agreement' via 'courier'. The contradicted text refers to 'Agreement' via 'courier'. The change introduces a contradiction because it redefines the returning document as 'Agreement' and the method as 'courier' without clarifying if 'Agreement' is the same as 'NDA', creating misaligned terminology that implies 'NDA' is no longer sufficient. Further, changing 'post' to 'courier' creates an inconsistency, as one part of the contract now specifies 'courier' for returning the 'Agreement', while another may still refer to 'post' for returning the 'NDA'. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition because the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.
